message.abstract:
<jats:title>
                  A
                  <jats:sc>bstract</jats:sc>
                </jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Purpose</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our objective was to assess adolescent’s Health-Related Quality of Life (HRQoL) and psychological distress, from their own and their parents’ perspective, and to examine associated risk factors during the COVID-19 pandemic in Geneva, Switzerland.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A random sample of adolescents, aged 14-17 years, and their families was invited to a serosurvey in November and December 2020. Adolescents’ HRQoL was evaluated using the validated adolescent-reported KIDSCREEN-10 and parent-reported KINDL
                    <jats:sup>®</jats:sup>
                    scales. Psychological distress was assessed with self-reported sadness and loneliness, and using the KINDL
                    <jats:sup>®</jats:sup>
                    emotional well-being scale. Risk factors for adolescents’ low HRQoL and psychological distress were identified using generalized estimating equations and both adolescents’ and their parents’ perceptions were compared.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Among 240 adolescents, 11% had a low HRQoL, 35% reported sadness and 23% reported loneliness. Based on parents’ perception, 12% of the adolescents had a low HRQoL and 16% a low emotional well-being. Being a girl (aOR=3.29; 95%CI: 1.64-6.57), increased time on social media (aOR=2.05; 95%CI: 1.08-3.88), parents’
                    <jats:italic>average to poor</jats:italic>
                    mood (aOR=2.81; 95%CI: 1.21-6.56) and
                    <jats:italic>average to poor</jats:italic>
                    household financial situation (aOR=2.30; 95%CI: 1.00-5.29) were associated with an increased risk of sadness. Mismatches between adolescents’ and their parents’ perception of HRQoL were more likely for girls (aOR
                    <jats:bold>=</jats:bold>
                    2.88; 95%CI: 1.54-5.41) and in households with lower family well-being (aOR
                    <jats:bold>=</jats:bold>
                    0.91; 95%CI: 0.86-0.96).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A meaningful proportion of adolescents experienced low well-being during the second wave of COVID-19. Adolescents living in underprivileged or distressed families seemed particularly affected. Monitoring is necessary to evaluate the long-term effects of the pandemic on adolescents.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Implications and Contribution</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study describes the psychological well-being of a population-based sample of adolescents in Geneva, Switzerland amid the COVID-19 pandemic, and identifies adolescents at risk of distress. This study provides further insight by comparing adolescents’ well-being as reported by themselves and their parents.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Health-Related Quality of Life and psychological distress of adolescents during the COVID-19 pandemic in Geneva
